data.title: Understanding VT: Vanguard's All-In-One Global Stock ETF
data.description:
What is VT?

Vanguard Total World Stock Index Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:VT) is an exchange‑traded fund that seeks to track the FTSE Global All Cap Index, thus mirroring nearly the entire global stock market. That coverage spans developed and emerging markets; it also includes large, mid, and small‑cap firms. Launched in June 2008, VT still provides broad equity exposure through a single, passively managed vehicle; moreover, stock selection involves minimal human intervention. At the same time, and true to Vanguard’s core philosophy, the fund carries an annual expense ratio of just 0.06%. Consequently, it remains one of the most cost‑effective ways to access global equities.

What Does VT Offer Investors?

VT delivers comprehensive global diversification by spreading investments across thousands of companies in numerous countries. Because the fund buys more of the biggest companies (measured by their total stock‑market value), a lot of its money ends up in U.S. stocks. Still, it doesn’t ignore the rest of the world; it puts a meaningful chunk into Europe, Asia, and fast‑growing developing countries. Spreading money around like this cuts risk because you’re not betting on just one region. That said, most of those holdings are in large, well‑established companies, not in smaller, more speculative firms.

When it comes to performance, VT has posted steady growth over time. Over the past year, it generated strong returns that tracked global equity markets; moreover, since inception, it has produced mid‑single‑digit average annualized growth, a figure that mirrors the long‑term upward trend of global markets. Volatility lines up with well‑diversified large‑cap equity benchmarks. There are occasional moderate drawdowns; nevertheless, the ride is generally smoother than what you find in single‑country funds.

VT keeps costs and taxes low. Because it’s an ETF, you can buy or sell it any time the market is open, and it usually doesn’t hand you big taxable payouts like many mutual funds do. Vanguard is huge, so the gap between its buying and selling prices is tiny, and there’s plenty of trading activity to get in or out easily. Lately, rising stock markets worldwide, especially in Europe and Japan, have boosted the fund, though U.S. shares still make up the biggest slice. Finally, Vanguard’s broader cost‑cutting move in early 2025 reduced fees across many funds; however, VT’s already ultra‑low fee stayed virtually unchanged.

Who Might Consider the Vanguard Total World Stock Index Fund?

Think of VT as an all‑in‑one stock fund. Instead of making you pick separate funds for the U.S., Europe, Asia, and so on, it bundles almost the entire world’s stock markets into a single package. That makes life easier if you just want broad coverage without doing extra homework. On top of that, it’s cheap to own and easy to buy or sell, so it works well both for people who plan to hold it for many years and for those who like to tweak their portfolios from time to time.

For investors who already hold concentrated positions, most commonly in U.S. equities, VT can, importantly, act as a broad complement. It spreads some of your investment into Europe, Asia, and other parts of the world where prices and market forces aren’t the same as in the U.S. This approach keeps you from stacking all your chips on a single bet. Conversely, if you’d rather drill down on something more precise, like fast‑growing small‑caps, dividend‑rich stocks, or one particular country, VT can seem too broad since it skims those areas instead of zeroing in on them.

What’s Inside VT? A Closer Look at Sector Allocation and its Top Holdings

To better understand how VT’s broad mandate translates into actual exposure, we’ll now take a closer look at its top five holdings and examine what each contributes to the fund’s overall structure.

1. Microsoft Corporation

Microsoft (MSFT) makes up about 3.7% of the VT fund, making it the fund’s single biggest stock holding. The company first became famous for Windows and Office, but under its current boss, Satya Nadella, it has reinvented itself. Today, it’s a major force in cloud computing (Azure), business software and social networking (Office 365 and LinkedIn), and video games (Xbox and its purchase of Activision Blizzard). Moreover, a decisive push into artificial intelligence, now fused through many of its platforms, illustrates Microsoft’s adaptability along with strong financial discipline, healthy profit margins, and steady shareholder returns via dividends and buybacks, keeping the stock at the core of many long‑term portfolios.

2. NVIDIA Corporation

Meanwhile, NVIDIA (NVDA) Corporation, at 3.45% of the fund, ranks just behind Microsoft in VT’s lineup, proving that a focused tech niche can become the backbone of today’s digital economy. Founded in 1993, the company started out making high‑end graphics chips for gamers, yet those same GPUs now drive AI projects, scientific modeling, and other serious high‑performance computing jobs. Because AI is booming, NVIDIA’s hardware now runs everything from huge data centers to self‑driving cars and big AI‑training systems. The market noticed: by mid-2025, the company’s total stock value briefly topped $4 trillion, an industry first, showing how central it has become. Under longtime CEO Jensen Huang, NVIDIA keeps moving fast and executing well, though its sky‑high price tag means the stock can swing up and down. Even so, its tech is so critical to the next wave of computing that VT gives it an important place in the fund.

3. Apple Inc.

Apple Inc. (AAPL) makes up about 3.23% of the fund. It contributes a different kind of tech leadership, one built on seamless ecosystem integration and strong consumer loyalty. Since launching in 1976, Apple has gone from personal computer pioneer to shaping how billions talk, work, and stream through the iPhone, iPad, Mac, and Apple Watch. Each device ties into services like Apple Music, iCloud, and the App Store, thus creating a cycle that keeps users and their subscription dollars coming back. Thanks to this setup, Apple’s sales keep growing. It generates plenty of cash, and it spends that money wisely. The catch is that a big chunk of its revenue still depends on iPhone sales. And its newer bets on augmented reality and artificial intelligence could be risky. Still, under CEO Tim Cook, Apple is an iconic brand that merges new ideas with solid day‑to‑day management, so many investors see it as a reliable, well‑known piece of VT’s portfolio.

4. Amazon.com, Inc.

Amazon (AMZN) makes up a little over 2% of the VT fund. It started as an online bookstore back in 1994, but now it does just about everything in tech and retail around the world. The online shopping site still brings in the most sales dollars, yet most of the company’s profits actually come from Amazon Web Services (AWS), its cloud computing business. AWS is the top provider of rented, on‑demand computing power for everyone from tiny startups to huge global companies. At the same time, Amazon keeps rolling out new ideas in package delivery, artificial intelligence, video streaming, and smart‑home gadgets, blending in‑store and online shopping. CEO Andy Jassy leads a company noted for reinvestment even at the expense of short‑term profits. Regulatory scrutiny, fierce competition, and economic sensitivity persist, but Amazon’s scale, infrastructure, and tech edge make it a key piece of VT’s global mix.

5. Meta Platforms, Inc.

Finally, Meta Platforms (META) makes up about 1.6% of the VT fund. You probably know it as the company behind Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, Messenger, and Threads, apps that connect billions of people and make most of their money by selling ads. Meta is also pouring a lot of cash into new tech like artificial intelligence and the “metaverse” through its Reality Labs division. These are big, expensive strategies that could pay off later. CEO Mark Zuckerberg keeps pouring resources into data infrastructure and AI despite public scrutiny, tighter rules, and tough competition. Even so, Meta’s flagship apps are woven into everyday life, so the company stays relevant, and that’s why VT holds a solid position in it.

VT Performance Overview

When people talk about its “momentum,” they’re really asking, “Has this ETF been gaining value faster than other funds and for how long?”

The answer is yes.

Over the last year, VT grew about 14.4% in total value. That’s roughly 74% better than the middle‑of‑the‑pack ETF.

Stretch the window to three years, and your money would have piled up about 61.8%; give it five years, and you’re looking at almost an 84% increase. In other words, the fund has been beating the average for quite a while.

How does it compare with the famous S&P 500 index of big U.S. companies?

Surprisingly well.

Over one, three, and five years, VT has kept pace. True, if you look way back over ten years, the S&P 500 still wins. But that long‑term gap doesn’t change the main point: owning a globally diversified fund like VT hasn’t forced investors to sacrifice growth. It’s still been a solid, long‑run choice.

The really impressive thing about VT isn’t just that it beat other funds once; it keeps doing so. Over the past one to three years, its price gains blew past the typical ETF by such a wide margin that Seeking Alpha gave it an A‑ for its most recent six‑month stretch, during which it outperformed the average fund by about 249%.

Why?

As previously noted, a lot of VT’s money is in big, fast‑growing companies around the world, especially in tech, and that blend has benefited from both a weaker U.S. dollar and the ongoing strength of large technology firms. At the same time, VT spreads its investments widely: only about 19% of its assets are tied up in its ten biggest holdings. Because the fund isn’t overloaded with just a few stocks, a bad earnings report from one company won’t torpedo the whole portfolio. That broad spread also keeps VT’s day‑to‑day price swings below the ETF average, so investors get a smoother ride whenever markets get volatile.

On the other hand, total return is not the only goal for every saver. Many savers, especially retirees, also want steady cash coming in. That cash usually arrives as dividends or interest. But when prices keep rising via inflation, a small dividend doesn’t go very far.

That’s where VT falls a bit short.

VT’s distribution yield is just 1.76%. Its three‑year compound dividend growth rate of 3.49% pales beside a peer median north of 8%, and its ten‑year record is equally subdued.

It's worth noting that VT has sent a dividend check every year for sixteen straight years, so you can count on getting something. Still, if you’re looking for bigger or faster‑growing income, this fund probably won’t impress you. You might decide to pick a different fund that pays more, even if that other fund charges higher fees or owns fewer kinds of stocks.

Fees can quietly eat away at your investment gains, but VT keeps them tiny. The fund charges just 0.06% a year, compared with about 0.50% for the average ETF, so it gets top marks for being cheap. You can also buy or sell it without much extra cost, because the difference between the buying price and selling price is only about 0.01%.

Even small fees compound over time. Trim roughly half a percentage point from your annual costs, and, a couple of decades down the line, the savings can be substantial. VT makes that easier: it turns over only about 3% of its portfolio each year, versus roughly 30% at a typical fund, so taxes and trading costs stay low. On top of that, it hugs its benchmark, deviating by less than 1% (tracking error for both one and five years), so you get the market’s return without unwanted surprises.

Should You Invest in VT?

Overall, Vanguard’s Total World Stock ETF offers everyday investors a simple, low‑cost way to own a slice of almost every major stock market, pairing respectable long‑term growth with below‑average volatility and razor‑thin fees that let compounding do the heavy lifting. Because it spreads your money across thousands of companies and never puts too much into any single one, it has grown nicely over the years without swinging wildly up and down, and it’s even kept pace with the famous S&P 500 during shorter stretches. The catch is that it doesn’t pay out much in dividends, so it won’t please anyone who needs steady income from their investments. But if you’re the type who just wants to buy a fund, leave it alone, and let low fees and long‑term growth do the work, VT can be a solid core holding, giving you room to add more specialized funds on the side if you want.

This article answers these three main questions about VT:

  1. Is VT a good ETF for diversifying a portfolio?
  2. Does VT appeal to income investors?
  3. Is VT more or less volatile than the S&P 500?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Christian Dior, Coca-Cola, Trinity Capital, Amgen And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today, we're looking at articles published between July 11–July 17.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

NANO Nuclear Energy: No DOME, No Problem

Jorge Hortelano | Buy

NANO Nuclear Energy (NNE) is a bet. The appeal of this investment idea lies in gaining exposure to a niche where publicly traded companies are few and far between—and that niche is micro-nuclear reactors.

The issue we’re facing is that the company’s products are still in a conceptual phase, it has no revenue, and, to make matters worse, it was recently announced that NNE won’t be participating in the Demonstration of Microreator Experiments [DOME] round scheduled for spring 2026. And yet, in my opinion, there are still enough reasons to assign a buy rating to this stock, despite the obvious risks involved.

Global Ship Lease: Attractive Yield Likely To Increase Further

Melissa Tucker | Buy

I have covered GSL before, where I outlined the investment thesis in detail and explained why I considered it a strong buy. Since then, the company has continued with its steady operations and reported Q1 figures that beat estimations. The stock has reached my previous fair value after rising more than 30% since my previous article, but I still consider it an interesting buy.

Why I'm A Buyer Of Christian Dior SE

Arjen Elgersma | Buy

I recently took a position in LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF)(OTCPK:LVMUY) through the holding company Christian Dior SE (OTCPK:CHDRF) (OTCPK:CHDRY) at a price of around €424 (Christian Dior SE price). At the moment I bought the Christian Dior SE shares, the share price of LVMH was around €444. Although I do think there are better companies around in the luxury sector, I still see a chance of achieving a good return with Christian Dior SE. In this analysis, I will explain why, but I will also share some doubts and criticisms.

As Christian Dior SE's only function is holding shares in LVMH, I will talk mainly about LVMH in this analysis and just used Christian Dior SE as a more attractive investment way to buy shares of LVMH.

Elevance Health: Much Cheaper Than UNH, Yet No Drama

Michael B Howard | Strong Buy

As stocks continue to go up and down endlessly, investors can often confuse these price changes as value opportunities. In reality, the market is pricing all future expected returns from the companies that make up the market, and based on news, politics, economics, social and other factors, the viability of previous earnings forecasts and expectations can easily come into question. As such, investors should be wary, not excited, about a stock price decline.

However, in the case of Elevance Health (NYSE:ELV), my analysis and thinking lead me to the conclusion that the market capitalization of the company is somewhat disjointed from the future earnings of the company – in particular, in the long run.

What has caused a massive decline in value across the health sector is multi-faceted, which I'll get to in this article.

Further, the earnings ratios have become incredibly cheap compared to historical valuations.

Coca-Cola: Warning Signs Are Flashing Red For Shareholders

Skeptical12 | Sell

In a market, premium almost always comes at a price. While some iconic and well-known companies such as Apple (AAPL) and Amazon (AMZN) have consistently been the best-performing stocks, other brands such as NIKE (NKE) and PepsiCo (PEP) have become overvalued. The added appeal of investing in famous brands often doesn't show up in the returns investors receive.

Coke remains one of the most well-recognized brands not just in the United States but also worldwide. The nearly $300 billion company sells into nearly 200 countries. Still, while Coca-Cola's (NYSE:KO) (NEOE:COLA:CA) brand power obviously remains strong, the leading beverage provider's investment returns have lagged the broader indexes for some time.

The Coca-Cola Company offered investors total returns of 136.9 percent over the last decade, while the S&P 500 offered investors total returns of 258.1 percent during this same time period.

I wanted to update my coverage of the company since the first quarter earnings report and the President's decision to enact reciprocal tariffs on a number of countries as part of a new trade policy. I last wrote about the Coca-Cola company in March of 2025, and I rated the company a sell. Today, I am reiterating my sell rating on Coca-Cola. The beverage maker relied heavily on unsustainable price increases over the last several years, and management has not been able to drive consistent and substantive organic volume growth. The company still looks overvalued relative to what are fairly modest growth expectations.

Harrow's Internal $100 Price Target Is Ambitious, But New Bloomberg Data Backs It Up

Michigan Value Investor | Strong Buy

Harrow, Inc. (NASDAQ:HROW) achieved its first revenue in 2015 as an ophthalmic compounding pharmacy and now dominates this tiny niche with a roughly 35% share, perhaps 10 times the size of its nearest competitor. Roughly half of ophthalmic drug prescribers are now HROW customers. Following the examples set by pharma giants Allergan and Alcon, which also began as small compounding pharmacies, HROW leveraged its existing customer base to pivot into the much higher-priced branded pharmaceutical space in early 2023. Business is absolutely booming, and HROW today can look back at 10 years of 40% CAGR revenue growth, and forward guidance is for this CAGR to actually increase through the end of the decade. I have previously written a deep dive on HROW, so anyone interested in a full description of the business model might want to check that out.

In this article, I discuss the implications of HROW's 2025 share-based compensation (SBC) management bonus structure, which pays no bonuses at all unless HROW's price moves up more than 50% from today's price ($31.75 as I write this) and pays out fully only if the share price moves up more than 200%. Next, I discuss their new drug, Byqlovi, which was acquired on June 9, 2025. And finally, I share an update on the performance of three of HROW's drugs using third-party data, which Bloomberg has reported growing in Q2 2025 by 30% to more than 60% compared to Q1 2025.

Crispr Therapeutics' Bright Future Is Coming Into Focus

Marc Gerstein | Buy

I first wrote about CRISPR Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CRSP), describing it as “the future of medicine,” on 6/10/24.

The company is a pioneer and leader in gene editing. It uses that protocol to create its own treatments. It also makes its platform available to other companies that work in this field. ...

... [T]his company does truly represent the future of medicine. Its treatments will, assuming they work, really cure disease.

And when I say cure, I mean CURE… for real.

Often, treatments don’t change the underlying physical condition. They just mask unpleasant symptoms as best they can. Gene editing, on the other hand, literally changes the (typically mutated) gene that causes the problem.

And freed from a corporate mandate to write newsletter buy-recommendations, I said “Hold” in my debut Seeking Alpha CRSP writeup.

For most of the time since my prior writeup, CRSP stock was down.

As of now, with a big rally (see below), the stock is about 5% lower since my 6/10/24 writeup. The S&P 500 is up about 17%.

Northrop Grumman Surprise Blow For F/A-XX After Boeing F-47 Win

Dhierin Bechai | Hold

Northrop Grumman (NYSE:NOC) has been on the short list to win the F/A-XX contract award, or at least I thought so following the NGAD (Next Generation Air Dominance) award to Boeing (BA). In a prior report, I detailed why I believed that with Boeing being awarded the NGAD contract, Northrop Grumman would be in pole position to win the F/A-XX contract. However, in recent weeks, the future of that program has been in doubt.

In this report, I discuss the challenges for the F/A-XX program and also explain why I believe a shift in the funding priority may be a mistake, a mistake that could still be rectified.

Correction Alert: Ares Capital Getting Risky

Samuel Smith | Sell

Ares Capital (NASDAQ:ARCC) is a leading blue-chip business development company (BIZD) that has a very impressive track record of delivering market-crushing total return performance over the long term.

I last wrote about ARCC back in February and rated it a Hold, citing several reasons why it did not present compelling value to investors at the time, including the fact that it charges some of the highest fees in the BDC space, its valuation multiple was quite high relative to its historic averages, and – while its base dividend was attractive and safe – it was unlikely to pay any supplemental dividends for the foreseeable future. Finally, there would likely be some net investment income headwinds in the future as there was yield compression taking place in the sector, and there was some evidence that non-accrual rates could be on the rise.

Since then, the stock has performed roughly in line with the broader market, thereby justifying the hold rating that I assigned to it. However, I think that the company's stock is getting increasingly risky, as I believe it is setting up for a meaningful correction in the not-too-distant future. In this article, I will detail why.

Trinity Capital: 14% Yield To Buy Now

Roberts Berzins, CFA | Buy

Back in November 2024, I wrote a piece about Trinity Capital Inc. (TRIN) and Hercules Capital, Inc. (HTGC), highlighting these both as potential candidates for investors, who want to consider opening some exposure towards VC BDCs.

In my view, TRIN and HTGC have the necessary margin of safety and, in general, an investment policy that is designed to be as prudent as it gets in the VC segment.

Since the publication of that piece, TRIN has registered ~17% in total returns (about 12% of the returns have come from dividends).

And what is quite interesting now is that TRIN is priced at P/NAV of 1.11x, which makes it as one of the cheapest internally managed BDCs. Plus, the current yield is 14.2% - i.e., by 220 basis points above the sector median.

So, the question is whether TRIN is a buy considering these attractive metrics, the recent earnings dynamics, and its overall substance of how it creates value.

Amgen: Great Starting Yield For This Wealth Compounder

Gen Alpha | Buy

It pays to have a moat around your business. The wider the moat, the better, as that’s what differentiates a brand or product versus that of others. Moats can be tenuous, however, especially in the consumer retail space due to changing consumer preferences. This has been the case with companies like V.F. Corporation (VFC), which overstretched itself and has had to retrench in the face of struggling brands like Vans.

That’s why I much rather prefer well-run pharmaceutical firms with patent protection, industry know-how, and a robust pipeline. I find the case with Amgen Inc. (NASDAQ:AMGN), which I last covered in March, highlighting its strong profitability and innovation pipeline. It appears the market has yet to agree, as AMGN has fallen in price by 3.4% since my last piece. As shown below, AMGN currently sits in the middle of its 52-week range with a forward PE of 14.2 and a 3.2% yield.

This is not a bad thing, however, as AMGN’s current valuation gives long-term investors an opportunity to add to this name while the market is busy chasing growth.

Sibanye Stillwater's Market Positioning Should Drastically Improve

Oakoff Investments | Buy

I've written 8 bullish articles on Sibanye Stillwater Limited (SBSW) stock here on Seeking Alpha. In my most recent take, dating back to August 2024, I argued that Sibanye seemed to have had great rebound prospects thanks to the internal cost-cutting efforts and the management team's focus on the green metals, which should have added some sort of diversification.

Although we can see that SBSW managed to rebound nicely since my previous update (it's up by 93% since August 2024), it's still underperforming the broader market by a meaningful margin (for the past few years).

A lot of time has passed since my previous article, and there are some strong fundamental factors driving the stock price higher since calendar Q2 2025. In addition to those factors, I see another one that may provide a massive positive catalyst for Sibanye in the next few years (at least), so hopefully the price action downtrend will finally break to the upside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TIP: Trump Feud With Federal Reserve May Collapse Bond Market
data.description:
Over the past two years, the bond market's concerns have seemingly shifted away from inflation toward volatility around fiscal stability and foreign Treasury demand. Of course, these issues are closely related, as a decline in perception of stability from the US Treasury could push demand lower, weakening the dollar and stoking inflation. In my opinion, President Trump's increased efforts to fire Jerome Powell and replace him with a more dovish leader may drive volatility in bond markets, specifically inflation-indexed Treasuries such as those in the iShares TIPS Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:TIP).

TIP offers investor exposure to US TIPS, a special class of Treasury bonds that are indexed to the consumer price index. The yield on these funds is lower, around 1.7% today, with the gap between it and non-indexed bonds like (IEF) usually attributable to inflation and inflation expectations. For example, IEF's yield to maturity is 4.36% while TIP's is 1.69%, telling us it is pricing for inflation around 2.67% (the difference) over the maturity period of (on average) seven years.

TIP outperforms when inflation rises faster than expected and vice versa. From 2020 to 2022, inflation rose faster than anticipated, leading to outperformance for TIP. Although inflation has slowed dramatically since, it has followed estimated levels closely, leading to roughly equal performance between it and IEF. See below:

TIP's yield after the CPI is called the "real interest rate" and is driven less by inflation and more by Treasury risk perception and Federal Reserve policy. In February, I published "TIP: Inflation Up, U.S. Fiscal Stability Down," explaining my view that fiscal debt pressures and associated monetary risks signaled by gold created risk for TIP. Real rates have remained stable since, with TIP having a 3.4% total return. However, with the passing of a deficit-increasing spending bill, greater concerns regarding Federal Reserve independence, and a falling US dollar, I suspect TIP may be due for a sharp change.

Fed Quietly Ending Monetary Tightening Policy

Financial media have discussed changes in interest rates at length; however, there has been far less discussion regarding the end to its balance sheet wind-down. In my view, the influence of roughly doubling the monetary base in 2020 on financial markets and inflation has been far greater than that of rate cuts and hikes. In general, when the Federal Reserve purchases Treasuries (adding new money to the financial system), we see real interest rates decline. Since 2022, the Federal Reserve has slowly removed some money from the financial system, pushing real rates higher by decreasing a major source of demand. See below:

The five-year real Treasury rate has sustained a negative channel since late 2023, creating a positive tailwind for TIP (directly inversely correlated to the real rate). However, the rate of the Federal Reserve's balance sheet wind-down of Treasuries has slowed dramatically in recent months.

Analysts expect the QT trend to end early next year and potentially sooner to reduce the risk of Treasury volatility amid the debt ceiling. The US Treasury had been at its debt ceiling since January, stopping the government's ability to increase borrowings, instead using Treasury cash and a mix of "extended measures" (such as not funding pensions and retirement accounts). This indirectly added at least $500B in liquidity to financial markets based on the decline in US Treasury cash:

With the passage of the "Big Beautiful Bill," increasing the limit by $5T, the Treasury is now free to pursue auctions necessary to improve its balance and fill unpaid accounts. Most likely, the total liquidity impact will be around $1T, given the 2024 deficit was $1.8T and interest rate spending and general spending have increased since; the $500B figure is based on the decline in Treasury cash, not accounting for extraordinary measures that slowed the cash hemorrhage.

With the Treasury free to issue new debt, its managers look to flood the market with around $900B to $1.6T in T-bills over the next year. Because the yield curve is steepened, I expect to see more debt issued at short-term rates, with the Treasury likely banking on rate cuts to lower costs. Of course, should inflation spike, increased reliance on short-term funding will accelerate the financing issue.

The US Treasury has $5.78T in bills outstanding, or debt due within a year; although that has slipped this year with the debt ceiling, it should rise much higher over the coming months as the Treasury pays not only higher spending levels but also bills it avoided over the past year, likely while refilling its cash reserves. This is ill-timed, as bank excess liquidity levels are very low based on the reverse repurchase level. See below:

Reverse repurchase agreements are liabilities for the Federal Reserve created when banks lend excess cash back to the Federal Reserve. I've followed this figure for years, as once it reaches sustained low levels, as it has since the end of 2024, the Federal Reserve must halt quantitative tightening or it will strain bank liquidity.

This ties into the excessive T-bills outstanding and the upcoming auction binge because that will also draw on liquidity reserves from the banking system. The Fed was able to pursue QT since 2022 because banks had so much cash coming out of quantitative easing that they could not deploy it through lending. Today, banks have an appropriate liquidity level, but that balance is likely to be altered over the second half of the year as the Treasury taps banks for more lending, while banks do not necessarily have sufficient liquidity to meet this demand.

The Federal Reserve is slowing the pace of tightening but has shown no interest in returning to quantitative easing. This creates a conundrum for bonds, specifically inflation-indexed bonds; an end to tightening should push real rates back down, pushing TIP up. However, because that is occurring due to a lack of excess liquidity (and a potential liquidity shortage), I argue the opposite is more likely, causing real rates to rise until private investment demand for Treasuries improves.

Historically, real rates and the US dollar are correlated because foreign investors seek yields greater than inflation and will prefer currencies with the best yields compared to expected inflation. When a few countries offer reasonable real interest rates, gold tends to rise, given that it is the last resort inflation hedge. See these relationships below:

The US dollar is weakening due to the decline in real rates and likely due to the potential for a very sharp decline in < 2-year real rates if the Federal Reserve cuts rates despite low unemployment and inflation potential.

As a result of potentially increased rate cuts, we've seen the two-year Treasury rate fall slightly, while the five-year inflation breakeven rate has risen quickly over the past few weeks:

These data signal increased inflation expectations, potentially attributable to lower rates. Of course, the short-term Treasury market is not free-market driven, being largely driven by Treasury and Federal Reserve activity, making them subpar gauges of rate expectations. Currently, the more accurate derivatives market looks to a 3.25–3.5% interest rate by July 2026 as most likely.

In the short term, these dynamics are likely positive for TIP. An end to QT and potentially larger rate cuts should push real rates lower, causing TIP to appreciate. TIP's duration is around 6 years, meaning a 1% decline in its yield should push it up by ~6%. I suspect real rates on medium-term TIP may fall by 50 bps with lower rates across the curve, potentially pushing TIP up around 3% over the coming months.

However, this continued dovish pivot is likely to stoke inflation, based on the trend in expectations. Again, that is good for TIP in the short term, but higher inflation and lower rate odds are pushing the dollar lower, decreasing market liquidity from foreign investors during an already tight liquidity environment. Assuming the Fed is more dovish, they may pursue (indirect, 2020-style) debt monetization in this scenario, returning to QE in order to shore up demand for Treasuries. That would likely boost TIP but leave it less stable, increasingly dependent on artificial financing from the Fed instead of private markets.

The Bottom Line

In the short term, such as the rest of 2025, my outlook on TIP is slightly bullish. The shift in Federal Reserve policy should result in lower real interest rates, reversing some of TIP's 2022 declines. This will potentially be accelerated if Powell is replaced with a very dovish chair, as Trump has stated.

However, looking beyond 2025, I expect TIP will eventually face another large leg lower. Much of this depends on whether or not the Fed is willing to buy longer-term Treasuries, which may be necessary to stabilize the Treasuries financing and provide banks with needed liquidity but would also further destabilize the US dollar exchange rate and promote inflation, potentially forcing the Fed back to hawkish policies. In short, I expect a repeat of the 2020-2022 scenario, but potentially with more extreme outcomes. Indeed, gold and silver may have risen significantly in expectation of this.

While TIP may seem like a hedge against inflation, its value can be compromised by a sharp and sustained rise in real interest rates. If private investors do not think the Treasury can sustainably meet its obligations, I expect the value of all associated assets to decline. In my view, we may see such declines with non-inflation-indexed Treasuries, particularly long-term bonds like (TLT) over the coming months. TIP's inflation-backing gives it hedge potential in the short term; however, once the US dollar falls sufficiently, I expect TIP will need to decline (pushing real rates higher) to restore foreign and private market demand.

data.title: VEGI: Overpriced And Overconcentrated
data.description:
It doesn't matter which niche market you want to invest in; there's probably an ETF for it. Today, we're going to cover one that focuses on agriculture, the iShares MSCI Agriculture Producers ETF (NYSEARCA:VEGI).

Investing in agriculture presents a compelling long-term investment case. Food is a basic need. The global population is expected to grow, so there will be increasing demand for agricultural products. Also, the sector can sometimes act as a buffer during broader economic downturns, as food enjoys a steady demand and agriculture tends to follow different cycles.

But over the years, I've learned that just because an investment case makes sense doesn't mean it's a great investment.

Therefore, we're going to dive deeper into VEGI's fundamentals to find out if it delivers on its promise to offer stability and promising long-term returns.

iShares MSCI Agriculture Producers ETF (VEGI)

But let's start with the basics. Here's how iShares describes the ETF on its website:

Investment Objective

The iShares MSCI Agriculture Producers ETF seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of global equities of companies primarily engaged in the business of agriculture.

Why VEGI?

1. Exposure to companies that produce fertilizers and agricultural chemicals, farm machinery, and packaged foods and meats.

2. Targeted access to global agricultural stocks

3. Use to diversify your portfolio and express a sector view

(iShares.com)

The net expense ratio is 0.39%, which is in line with other ETFs that track the performance of a single sector. The turnover rate is low at 7%, and the ETF rarely changes its holdings.

P/E Ratio

As a value investor, I put special emphasis on valuations. Yahoo Finance claims the average P/E ratio is 19.42, while the Wall Street Journal (WSJ) has it at 27.21. I usually rely on the WSJ numbers, as they tend to reflect earnings multiples on a TTM basis quite accurately.

Dividend Yield

The dividend yield is 2.39% at current valuations. That's pretty disappointing for an ETF that focuses on the primary sector of the economy, where sustained high-growth rates aren't expected.

Top Holdings

VEGI holds 158 stocks, which for a niche ETF seems diversified. But the top ten holdings represent 61.27% of total assets — that's overly concentrated in just a few names.

For example, VEGI invests 26.22% of its assets in one single stock, Deere & Co. (DE). Don't get me wrong, as a concentrated value investor, I am eager to bet large on my best ideas, but if I were to invest in ETFs, I'd want to have as much diversification as possible. Isn't that the whole idea of passive investing anyway?

The Cyclicality of Agriculture

The high concentration becomes even more dangerous when you add cyclicality.

The overall demand for food in the world is noncyclical. We humans have to eat, and that won't change, even with GLP-1 drugs or AI entering the world.

Therefore, one could assume that an ETF like VEGI should provide steady cash flows and distributions, but that's not the case. While the packaged food industry historically has seen relatively predictable cash flows, the agricultural industry follows boom-and-bust cycles. And it's reflected in VEGI's dividend payments.

The companies in the agricultural industry mostly sell into a global commodity market and have little influence over pricing, while packaged food companies can charge stable and increasing prices with healthy margins due to branding.

Many of VEGI's holdings operate on a tight margin; a little swing in prices leads to high volatility in profits and cash flows.

Sure, demand for basic agricultural commodities is relatively stable. People don't eat twice as much just because grains are cheaper this year.

But the supply can fluctuate wildly based on weather, politics, and global events. This cyclicality leads to erratic cash flows and volatility in the share prices. Add VEGI's heavy concentration in just a few names, and it becomes clear that this ETF shouldn't be a major part of a well-diversified portfolio.

Deere & Co. Valuation

As a value investor, I put special emphasis on having a margin of safety. How do you calculate the intrinsic value of a commodity producer when visibility into future cash flows is difficult to attain? By averaging out the cash flows and earnings over the cycles.

Below is a historical chart of DE's FCF/share, VEGI's largest holding, with 26% of assets invested.

Just by looking at the chart, we get a sense of how cyclical and capital-intensive DE's business is.

Sure, the company has a great brand that's globally recognized and trusted. It has higher margins than most in the sector, but nonetheless, it whips with the cycles just like any other company in the sector. Farmers are less willing to buy a new tractor if the business outlook is deteriorating, even if the tractor is powered by AI.

DE tends to have relatively high capital expenditures (which makes it a less attractive long-term holding); therefore, to value the company, we're going to use net earnings.

In recent years, the company generated $20 to $30 EPS. Analysts expect $18 to $27 over the coming years. Given that analysts often tend to overestimate the earnings power of cyclical companies (never seen analysts rightly predict a cycle peak), I'd be cautious about relying on those estimates. But let's stay positive and say DE will generate on average $25 EPS in the coming years.

Right now, the shares are trading at $500, for a forward P/E ratio of 20. That's a steep price for a cyclical company with erratic cash flows. Given that agricultural commodity prices have come down substantially since 2022, I believe the valuation is outright dangerous.

Corteva Valuation

But DE is only one holding, a large one to be clear, so let's check the second-largest holding, Corteva Inc. (CTVA). Together, they represent 36.22% of total assets invested. CTVA specializes in seeds and crop protection products.

Since the spinoff from DuPont in 2019, the share price has almost tripled—but did the cash flows?

Cash flows and earnings have whipsawed, just like you'd expect from a cyclical company with relatively thin margins. The company has a 0.9% dividend yield and is trading at a P/E ratio of 60. An insane valuation for a cyclical company with relatively low margins.

Analysts expect the P/E ratio to fall to "just" 25x earnings next year, but as a conservative investor, I am not relying on it.

Comparison Against Other ETFs

Over the past few years, ETFs focused on agriculture performed similarly on a total return basis.

The Invesco DB Agriculture Fund (DBA) is slightly ahead of VEGI, but the volatility has been lower, at least over the last 5 years. If we view it on a longer timeframe, it becomes clear that DBA doesn't look too good.

DBA investors saw flat nominal returns on a total return basis, while VEGI investors gained 103%. VEGI's largest holding, DE, had a good run over the last decade.

DBA holds commodity futures, and historically, agricultural commodity prices haven't seen a sustained large price increase over the long term, due to technological advances. I don't expect it to change, but we'll see some price spikes due to temporary shortages. It doesn't make DBA a good long-term bet, though.

If I had to choose between VEGI and DBA, I'd pick VEGI without even thinking about it. VEGI holds income-producing companies, instead of just the underlying commodities. Income-producing assets beat non-productive ones over the long term.

That said, VEGI isn't cheap. Its largest holdings seem vastly overpriced, given that they operate on relatively thin margins in a highly cyclical sector. Add the high concentration in just a few names, and I can't help but rate it a "sell".

Conclusion

On paper, VEGI looks like a solid thematic play to one of our most essential basic needs: food. But when you dive deeper, its prospects are less promising.

VEGI is highly concentrated in just a few stocks; its largest holding represents 26% of total assets and is trading at a steep valuation. Agriculture is known for thin margins and high cyclicality; those aren't the characteristics of an outperforming long-term investment.

Sure, the long-term demand story behind agriculture is compelling, but VEGI seems risky, and the current valuations don't compensate for the risks they add to it. I rate it a "sell".

data.title: Credit Rating For The Unrated REITs (Part 11): Gladstone Land Corporation
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

We continue with this short article, our series on credit ratings for the unrated real estate investment trust with Gladstone Land Corporation (NASDAQ:LAND).

Company Overview

Gladstone Land Corporation was founded in 1997, and it is headquartered in Virginia, US. The company is engaged in buying agricultural land and leasing it. It owns 150 farms on around 103,000 acres across 15 states and holds over 55,000 acre-feet in California. The total assets of the company are close to $1.2 billion at the end of the first quarter of 2025, and the current market capitalization is close to $371 million.

At the time of writing, the common stock of LAND is trading at a market price of $10.26, with a 5.46% yield.

The forward P/FFO is 21.74. The forward funds from operations yield comes at 4.6%. Below are the basic valuation metrics of the stock:

Below, you can see the capital structure of the company:

The company has an asset coverage ratio of 229%. LAND earns about 23.30% gross in rental agricultural lands. The operating expenses, excluding depreciation, represent approximately 23.4% of the total revenue. The interest expenses are 4.4% of the debt. When calculating the asset yield (by dividing total revenues by total asset value), we obtain a value of 6.37%. However, for a more accurate assessment, a market-based adjustment is applied using the price/book value ratio, which is currently 0.54. After the market adjustments, the calculated asset yield is 8.47%, and the market-adjusted asset coverage ratio is close to 172.33%.

Preferred stocks

LAND has three preferred stocks:

All three are traded below par at the moment, and all are not rated by the major credit agencies like S&P, Moody's, and Fitch. All of them have their call dates passed, so they can be called at any time.

Gladstone Land Corporation 6% SER C CUM PFD (NASDAQ:LANDP) is priced at $20.02 currently and its current yield is 7.5%. It pays cumulative distributions of 6% per annum, or $1.50 annual amount, in the form of $0.125 monthly.

Gladstone Land Corporation 6.00% CUM REDEEMABLE PFD STK SER B (NASDAQ:LANDO) has a current market price of $19.98 and a current yield of 7.51%. It pays cumulative distributions of 6% per annum, or $1.50 annual amount, in the form of $0.125 monthly.

Gladstone Land Corporation 5.000% CUM PFD D (NASDAQ:LANDM) is priced $24.86 at the time of writing, and its current yield is 5.02%. Its yield to maturity is on 01/31/2026, and it has a yield to maturity of 6.50%. It pays cumulative distributions of 5% per annum, or $1.25 annual amount, in the form of $0.104167 monthly.

Credit rating

Now let's look at the company's creditworthiness. Most of you know that we use Moody's methodology for credit rating companies. We don't follow it strictly, but with some adjustments, we arrive at the following results for LAND:

1. Gross assets: The total assets of LAND are $2 billion, so the company falls into a Ba2 equivalent rating here.

2. Unencumbered assets: Approximately 13% of its assets are unencumbered by collateral, giving it the worst rating of Ca3.

3. Secured debt: The level of secured debt is around 34% of the total assets, and it is equivalent to a B2 credit rating.

4. Total debt + preferred stock as a percentage of gross assets gives us several 70% and a credit rating score equivalent to B3.

5. Net debt to EBITDA: Net debt to EBITDA. We use the total debt here, and with an EBITDA of $67.4 million, the ratio is close to 15.05. This is again the bad Ca3 credit score.

6. Fixed charge coverage. EBITDA/(interest and dividend payment on its preferred stock). LAND gets a score of Caa3 here.

Based on these six "balance sheet" scores, LAND gets a balance sheet score of 17.72, or a Caa2 credit score.

Market Positioning and Asset Quality

But the market positioning receives a good score of A1 for this:

Operating environment

Agribusiness is less cyclical, meaning that agricultural land has a more stable value than, for example, commercial or office properties. Land also retains value in high inflation. The average remaining duration of contracts is 6.7 years, providing predictable cash flows. All this leads again to a good credit rating of the operating environment, equivalent to A1.

Liquidity and Access to Capital

The operating expenses of the business, excluding amortization, are close to 23.4%. EBITDA covers the interest expenses and preferred stock dividends around 1.4 times.

After calculating a score for market positioning, operating environment, and liquidity and adjusting all ratios based on the market-adjusted characteristics, we get a credit score of 14.85, which is equivalent to a B2 credit score.

Conclusion

LAND has a diversified portfolio of agricultural land and long-term leases. However, financial indicators – such as high debt to EBITDA, low fixed cost coverage, and limited unencumbered assets – worsen its overall credit rating. According to Moody's adapted methodology, LAND's market-adjusted credit score is B2, which indicates high risk, and it is better to refrain from its financial instruments, even if they have attractive yields.

data.title: Trump 1.0 Vs. 2.0: Back On Track?
data.description:
Back on April 8th, the S&P 500 (SP500) was down 16.9% in the 55 trading days since Inauguration Day on January 20th, but as shown below, the index has recovered all of its initial losses and is currently up 5% since President Trump re-took office. What a comeback!

At this point in Trump's first term back in July 2017, the S&P was sitting on a gain of 8% since Inauguration Day.

Back in 2017, we saw stocks pull back a bit in August but then explode higher from September through December, and the S&P ended up posting a gain of 18% from Inauguration Day 2017 through the end of that year. We'll have to wait and see what the rest of 2025 has in store, but either way, the market has seen an absolutely remarkable comeback from the depths of the Tariff Crash just a few months ago.

Even with the S&P back to new highs and up 5% since Trump's 2nd term began, the US is near the bottom of the pack in terms of country by country stock market performance since Inauguration Day. Below is an updated look at the performance of 45 country ETFs traded on US exchanges since Inauguration Day as well as since Trump's "Liberation Day" tariff announcements in the White House Rose Garden on April 2nd.

The average country ETF is up 16.8% since Trump re-took office, so the US (NYSEARCA:SPY) is underperforming that average by more than ten percentage points. The US is also still the worst performing of the G7 country ETFs since Inauguration Day, although it's up the fourth most of the G7 countries since April 2nd.

There are six country ETFs that are up 30%+ since Inauguration Day back in January: Greece (GREK), Poland (EPOL), Spain (EWP), Austria (EWO), South Korea (EWY), and Vietnam (VNAM). On the other end of the spectrum, there are five country ETFs in the red since Inauguration Day: Indonesia (EIDO), Thailand (THD), Argentina (ARGT), Turkey (TUR), and Saudi Arabia (KSA).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Downsides Of The AI Revolution
data.description:
The AI revolution and the massive number of financial headlines this paradigm shift has generated in recent years has been a huge driver of the stock market since the beginning of 2023. A lion's share of the gains in the market has come from the Magnificent Seven with AI related names like Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT) and Alphabet (GOOG).

And indeed, the AI revolution has triggered a huge explosion in spending. Big Tech alone is estimated to have spent $240 billion in 2024 towards building new AI data centers and capabilities. This surge of spending has helped power the markets to all-time highs despite a tepid global economy.

Thanks to this AI driven rally, equities are selling at extreme valuation measures using historical valuation metrics (price to sales ratio, Shiller PE, Market cap to GDP ratio, etc..).

Now the AI revolution has the potential to have the same impacts on the economy as the birth of the Internet a quarter of century ago according to many pundits. AI is predicted to provide a huge boost to productivity across the globe. Much has been written about these potential positive impacts. These are nicely summarized from this recent take by Brookings.

Based on current market valuations, this needs to happen for equities to maintain their current levels, let alone rally further. The S&P 500 (SP500) currently is producing nearly record profit margins historically. And these profit margins are facing increasing headwinds from both new tariff policies as well as tepid global growth. AI is going to have to be able to deliver significant productivity improvements to maintain current profit margins in the current environment.

Plenty of ink has been spilt on the benefits of the AI Revolution. This has been a key theme for the market rally since late 2022, and as previously stated a good portion of which has been driven by the Magnificent Seven. This article will focus more on the potential downsides of this paradigm shift and the possible impacts on the economy and markets. However, there are some large potential challenges and downsides to this massive paradigm shift. Here are some that should be on top of mind:

Electricity & Water Demand:

The huge AI data centers that are being built for the AI Revolution require a huge amount of electricity. Goldman Sachs was out with the projection earlier this year that data center demand for electricity will rise by 50% by 2027 and up to 165% by the end of this decade.

How will all this new electricity demand be met? Renewable sources like solar and wind do not offer the reliability data centers need. Coal is dirty and continues to decline as a source for electricity generation in the States and Europe. Nuclear is ideal in many ways. However, new nuclear capacity takes many years and in most cases more than a decade to bring online.

Natural gas generation seems the primary way this new demand for electricity will be met. The U.S. has huge reserves of natural gas and some 2.8 million miles of pipeline across the nation. This should be a long-term tailwind for natural gas producers like APA Corporation (APA) and Devon Energy (DVN) among other concerns. Companies with large natural gas pipelines like Kinder Morgan (KMI), Energy Transfer LP (ET) and Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD) should also be long-term beneficiaries.

Then there are the impacts to water demand and the environment. Global AI demand is projected to consume between 1.1 and 1.7 trillion gallons of freshwater annually by 2027 according to Investopedia. This is equivalent to thirty-four billion gallons of water per week. To put in perspective, an Olympic sized swimming pool holds 660,000 gallons of water.

One of the other downsides to the AI revolution is it is crowding out of funding for other types of businesses by venture capital as you can see above. These may turn out to be great investments for the VC industry, but that means less money available to be invested in other parts of the economy like biotech and fintech. This reallocation of capital will result in fewer companies and jobs being created in other parts of the economy. Not to mention, the innovation potential that will be lost or postponed.

Job Market Impacts:

Obviously, the hundreds of billions of dollars going to build out AI data centers will provide a one-time boost to construction jobs as these facilities get built. Anyone with AI skills will be in high demand as well. But what about a good portion of the white-collar workforce?

A whole slew of jab categories has been deemed very vulnerable to being replaced over the coming years. These include jobs like administrative assistants, executive secretaries, office clerks, filing and records management staff, customer service representatives, retail clerks, cashiers and checkout clerks, ticket clerks, inventory managers, fraud and other entry level analysts among many job classifications that could see significant changes over the near-term horizon.

Back in 2023, the World Economic Forum predicted AI will displace nearly 90 million workers globally by 2027. Recently, Goldman Sachs projected 300 million workers worldwide will be discharged due to AI by 2030. A 200-person law or CPA firm could well need 120 people or less to run the same firm by 2027 thanks to AI. This is just one example of how these types of job losses could play out through the economy.

Can new jobs be created fast enough to replace the ones being lost to AI over the coming few years? I have my significant doubts. What impacts will this have on consumer spending and consumer confidence? This is critical given consumers drive nearly 70% of U.S. economic activity. How will the debt-ridden governments of the G7 respond to the huge disruption in the jobs markets?

With the markets at record highs and priced at extreme valuations based on many historical valuation metrics, an investor has to believe among other things that the benefits of AI will outweigh the disruptions this revolution will have on myriad fronts. Color me skeptical for now on that front.
